title: Choosing Bottleneck Dimensions
concept: Bottleneck size is a key design choice, balancing compression strength against reconstruction fidelity. Boss explain ends with: 'Next up: Reconstruction Loss.'

exercises.prompt: You're packing for a trip with only a small backpack instead of a huge suitcase. What's the tradeoff?

options.text: You must pack only the most important items, so you might leave some things behind
You can pack everything you own with no limits
The size of your bag doesn't matter at all

exercises.explain: A smaller bag forces tough choices — you keep only what matters most. That's exactly what a small bottleneck does to data!

cards.title: How Narrow Should the Bridge Be?
cards.body: Imagine squeezing a whole city's traffic through one bridge. Too narrow, and cars get stuck. Too wide, and you didn't really save any space. Autoencoders face this exact puzzle with their bottleneck!

cards.title: What is Bottleneck Size?
cards.body: The bottleneck size is the number of values (dimensions) used to squeeze data into its smallest, most compressed form inside an autoencoder. Choosing this size is a balancing act: too small loses important details, too large barely compresses anything.

cards.title: The Suitcase Dilemma
cards.body: A tiny suitcase (small bottleneck) forces you to pack only essentials — light, but you might forget your toothbrush. A giant suitcase (large bottleneck) fits everything, but it's heavy and defeats the purpose of packing light. The best size depends on your trip!

cards.title: Compressing Faces
cards.body: Say you're building an autoencoder for 1000-pixel face photos. A bottleneck of 2 dimensions might squash all faces into blurry blobs. A bottleneck of 500 dimensions keeps faces sharp but barely compresses the data. A bottleneck of 50 might be the sweet spot: recognizable faces with real compression.

cards.title: Myth: Bigger Bottleneck Is Always Better
cards.body: Not true! A bigger bottleneck means less compression and less forced learning. Sometimes a smaller bottleneck actually helps the model learn the most important patterns instead of just copying everything.

cards.title: Small vs Large Bottleneck
cards.body: Choosing bottleneck size means balancing compression power against how well you can rebuild the original.

left.text: Strong compression, but reconstructions may be blurry or missing details

right.text: Weak compression, but reconstructions stay sharp and accurate

exercises.prompt: What happens if you make the bottleneck size too small?

options.text: The autoencoder loses important details and reconstructions get worse
The autoencoder automatically becomes faster to train with no downsides
The output image becomes larger than the input

exercises.explain: A tiny bottleneck forces heavy compression, which can throw away details needed to rebuild the original well.

exercises.prompt: A larger bottleneck always gives better compression than a smaller one.
exercises.explain: It's backwards! A larger bottleneck means LESS compression, since more values are kept. Smaller bottlenecks compress more strongly.

exercises.sentence: Choosing bottleneck size means balancing strong compression against ___ of the reconstruction.
exercises.explain: Fidelity means how close the reconstruction is to the original. Small bottlenecks risk lower fidelity in exchange for stronger compression.

exercises.prompt: Which bottleneck choice would likely produce the BLURRIEST reconstructed images?

options.text: A very small bottleneck, like 2 dimensions, for complex 1000-pixel images
A bottleneck almost as large as the original input size
A bottleneck chosen to match the complexity of the data

exercises.explain: Squeezing complex, detailed images into just 2 dimensions forces the model to throw away too much information, leading to blurry results.

exercises.prompt: You're designing an autoencoder for simple 10-pixel black-and-white doodles. Which bottleneck size is the SMARTEST choice?

options.text: A tiny bottleneck like 3 dimensions, roughly matching the doodle's real complexity
A bottleneck of 10,000 dimensions to be extra safe
No bottleneck at all, just copy the pixels directly
A random bottleneck size with no thought about the data

exercises.explain: Since simple doodles don't have much complexity, a small bottleneck that roughly matches their real information content compresses well while still reconstructing them accurately. Bigger isn't better here — it just wastes space without helping. Next up: Reconstruction Loss.
